@graph.name: Mercedes S-Class S63 AMG Engine Era Map
Mercedes S-Class S63 AMG Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S‑Class S63 AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M156 V8 typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only; the M157 costs £6,500–£11,000; and the M177 costs £7,500–£13,500. A used M156 engine costs on average £5,614. A used M157 engine with transmission can cost around £3,570. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. A rear main seal repair on the M177 can cost up to £7,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your S63 AMG.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S63 AMG engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which generation your S63 is. The W221's M156 (2006–2010) is a legendary naturally aspirated V8 but has head bolt and camshaft weaknesses. The M157 twin‑turbo (2011–2017) is more powerful but has oil leak and timing chain watchpoints. The M177 (2018+) is considered the more reliable of the three, but oil separator and rear main seal leaks are common. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the S63 AMG engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: M156 (W221 2006–2010): head bolt failure, camshaft wear, oil leaks. M157 (W221 2011–2013 & W222 2014–2017): oil leaks, timing chain wear, cylinder scoring. M177 (W222 2018–2020 & W223 2021+): oil separator failure → rear main seal leaks, software glitches, cooling system issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an S63 AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. A W221 S63 AMG is a £10,000–£25,000 vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 24–70% of the car's value. A W222 S63 AMG is a £20,000–£45,000 vehicle — a £7,500–£9,000 recon is 17–45% of the car's value. A W223 S63 AMG is a £40,000–£80,000+ vehicle — a £9,000–£11,000 recon is 11–28% of the car's value, making replacement a clear decision. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes S63 AMG engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The S63 AMG covers M156 (W221, 2006–2010), M157 (W221 2011–2013 & W222 2014–2017), and M177 (W222 2018–2020 & W223 2021+). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on an S63 AMG?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M156: have the head bolts replaced with upgraded units — this is the single most important investment you can make. Listen for ticking from the engine — camshaft wear indicator. On M157: regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil and shortened intervals (5,000–7,500 miles) are essential. On M177: have the oil separator inspected at 40,000–60,000 miles — replacing it early prevents expensive rear main seal leaks. At the first sign of coolant loss, ticking, cold‑start rattle or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes S‑Class S63 AMG Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above. A replacement M156 engine costs on average £5,614. A used M157 engine with transmission can cost around £3,570. A rear main seal repair on the M177 can cost up to £7,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S63 AMG engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The twin‑turbo V8's hot‑vee layout requires significant disassembly.

problems.title: Head Bolt Failure (M156 — W221, 2006–2010)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from exhaust, rough idle, engine warning light, external coolant leaks.
problems.repairCost: £2,000–£5,000 (head bolt replacement with upgraded bolts)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£8,500 (full engine replacement if head lifts and causes extensive damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or overheating, stop driving and book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M156's head bolts are the most significant weakness — the bolts can stretch or break, allowing the cylinder head to lift and coolant to leak. If left unaddressed, the engine can suffer catastrophic failure. Mercedes‑Benz issued a technical service bulletin for this issue. If you own an M156 S63 AMG, checking for head bolt issues is essential. Upgraded head bolts are available as a permanent fix. At the first sign of coolant loss or overheating, have the engine inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Vapor Separator Failure → Rear Main Seal Leak (M177 — W222 2018–2020 & W223 2021+)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, oil residue on the engine, low oil pressure warning, oil consumption.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£7,000 (oil separator replacement, rear main seal)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £7,500–£13,500 (full engine replacement if oil starvation causes damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice oil spots or burning smell

problems.recommendation: The M177's oil separator is a known weak point — if it fails, it can cause excessive crankcase pressure, leading to rear main seal (RMS) leaks. Some owners have reported $7,000 repairs for this issue. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected immediately — addressing the oil separator early prevents expensive RMS replacement.

problems.title: Camshaft Wear (M156 — W221, 2006–2010)
problems.symptoms: Ticking noise from the engine when cold, reduced power output, poor fuel economy, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £2,500–£5,000 (camshaft and lifter replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£8,500 (full engine replacement if damage is extensive)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear ticking from the engine

problems.recommendation: Premature wear of the camshaft lobes is a common problem with the M156 engine. This issue often manifests as a ticking noise from the engine. The primary cause is inadequate lubrication, often due to oil starvation or sludge buildup. Regular oil changes with high‑quality synthetic oil are essential to prevent camshaft wear. If you hear a ticking noise, have the camshafts and lifters inspected immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: S63 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace oil separator early — prevents expensive RMS failure
Catch it early — before cam lobes are scored
Catch it early — before chain snaps
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * M156 (W221 2006–2010): head bolts replaced with upgraded units — no ticking from the engine
* M157 (W221 2011–2013 & W222 2014–2017): no cold‑start rattle — timing chain in good condition
* M177 (W222 2018–2020 & W223 2021+): no oil leaks — oil separator and rear main seal in good condition
* Full Mercedes‑AMG service history with regular oil changes
avoidIf: * M156: coolant loss with no diagnosis — head bolts may be failing — walk away
* M156: ticking from the engine — camshaft wear may be present
* M157: cold‑start rattle with no chain history — timing chain issues likely
* M177: oil spots under the vehicle — oil separator or rear main seal may be failing
* Poor service history — AMG V8s are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S63 AMG is the performance flagship of the S‑Class range — a 525–612 hp V8 with genuine supercar‑rivalling performance. The W221's M156 is a legendary naturally aspirated V8 but has head bolt and camshaft weaknesses. The M157 twin‑turbo V8 is more powerful but has oil leak and timing chain watchpoints. The M177 is the most modern and reliable but has oil separator and rear main seal issues. Buy on full service history, check for coolant loss (M156), cold‑start rattle (M157) and oil leaks (M177), and never ignore a warning light. A well‑maintained S63 AMG is a thrilling, long‑lived flagship; a neglected one is a £10,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare S63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes S‑Class S63 AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (M157 timing chain) or ticking (M156 camshaft wear)
* Coolant Level (M156) — Check for unexplained loss; head bolt failure indicator
* Oil Leaks (M177) — Check under the car and around the engine; oil separator / RMS leaks
* Service History — including which generation (W221/W222/W223) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Head Bolt History (M156) — Check if upgraded bolts installed
* Timing Chain History (M157) — Check if chain inspected or replaced
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
